coredata.dc:description: Biofouling has become a concerning issue in developing thin-film composite (TFC) forward osmosis (FO) membranes. The attachment of microorganisms onto the TFC FO membrane surface seriously influenced the membrane life span, resulting in the increased operation and maintenance costs. Herein, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots using isophthalic acid (IPA) as a carbon source (IPA-C-dots) and nanocomposites by conjugation of IPA-C-dots with polylysine (PLL) (IPA-C-dots/PLL) were synthesized. The TFC FO membranes were coupled with nothing, PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL to prepare control membrane (Control-M), PLL one (PLL-M), and IPA-C-dots/PLL one (IPA-C-dots/PLL-M), respectively. Due to the excellent hydrophilicity and smoothness of the membrane surface, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M shows much higher water permeability, lower structural parameter, and acceptable salt rejection, resulting in enhanced membrane properties. What is more, the static and dynamic bio-fouling resistant tests indicate IPA-C-dots/PLL-M possesses a significant anti-biofouling property. The reason for this observation lay in the coupled IPA-C-dots can generate singlet oxygen (1O2) through energy transfer from excited triplet state to dissolved triplet oxygen under light irradiation, which can be served as a photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy reagent, bettering the biofouling resistance properties. The conjugation of RTP C-dots onto TFC FO membranes opened up a promising door for the high efficient inhibition of bacterial proliferation and the enhancement of membrane properties.

head.ce:title: Coupling room-temperature phosphorescence carbon dots onto active layer for highly efficient photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy and enhanced membrane properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biofouling has become a concerning issue in developing thin-film composite (TFC) forward osmosis (FO) membranes. The attachment of microorganisms onto the TFC FO membrane surface seriously influenced the membrane life span, resulting in the increased operation and maintenance costs. Herein, room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots using isophthalic acid (IPA) as a carbon source (IPA-C-dots) and nanocomposites by conjugation of IPA-C-dots with polylysine (PLL) (IPA-C-dots/PLL) were synthesized. The TFC FO membranes were coupled with nothing, PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL to prepare control membrane (Control-M), PLL one (PLL-M), and IPA-C-dots/PLL one (IPA-C-dots/PLL-M), respectively. Due to the excellent hydrophilicity and smoothness of the membrane surface, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M shows much higher water permeability, lower structural parameter, and acceptable salt rejection, resulting in enhanced membrane properties. What is more, the static and dynamic bio-fouling resistant tests indicate IPA-C-dots/PLL-M possesses a significant anti-biofouling property. The reason for this observation lay in the coupled IPA-C-dots can generate singlet oxygen (1O2) through energy transfer from excited triplet state to dissolved triplet oxygen under light irradiation, which can be served as a photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy reagent, bettering the biofouling resistance properties. The conjugation of RTP C-dots onto TFC FO membranes opened up a promising door for the high efficient inhibition of bacterial proliferation and the enhancement of membrane properties.

ce:def-description.ce:para: the numbers of colonies corresponding to the Control-M
the numbers of colonies corresponding to the modified membranes

ce:section.ce:para: Given an increase in global water scarcity, forward osmosis (FO), as an emerging osmosis-driven membrane process, has been rapidly increasing the applications in industrial and environmental wastewater purification [1–7]. FO shows advantages including low membrane fouling [8–12], environmental friendliness, and higher water flux recovery [13]. Therefore, many contributions have been made to the development of a variety of FO membranes with desirable characteristics. Among these, polyamide thin-film composite (PA-TFC) FO membranes have attracted much attention in water treatment due to their good chemical stability and high rejection to monovalent salt [5,14]. However, low water permeability and high membrane fouling still remain critical hindrances to the development of TFC FO membranes [6,15–18]. Fouling frequently takes place during long-term operation, especially biofouling, which is one of the most frequently happens and difficult to control, even though high levels of microbes are removed from the feed water. Most unfortunately, the water productivity of the membranes is generally not recoverable due to the irreversibility of biofouling. As the chief culprit of various membrane fouling problems, once the biofilm is stabilized on the membrane surface, the strong bacterial adhesion onto the surface and the sticky extracellular polymeric substance matrix formation resulted in an irreversible decrease in the permeate flux and salt rejection [19–22]. Therefore, many approaches, such as recovery of water flux via rinsing [23], improved fouling removal by air-sparging [24,25], osmotic backwashing [26,27], chemical cleaning [20], and imparting membranes with inhibiting bacterial proliferation ability [28–32], etc., have been explored to control biofilm growth and biofouling development. Though all of these methods can achieve the biofouling mitigation, the most efficient, convenient, and feasible antifouling strategy is the surface modification [22], which has a persistent demand.

Carbon dots (C-dots) as an emerging class of photoluminescent nanomaterials own unique photophysicochemical properties. As well as the predominant short-lived C-dots, afterglow ones have also been widely explored for metal-free alternatives to activate room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) [33–35]. On the one hand, during carbonization, the aromatic CN(S)/O or C–P(S) = O groups with n-π* transition absorption and emission self-brought by precursors or oxidation reaction bestrewed the surface of C-dots. Once the n-π* emissive centers were immobilized by various polymer matrices, the RTP of the C-dots was therefore observed [33–36]. On the other hand, these groups were embedded into the interior of C-dots, which can activate the matrix-free RTP through a polymer-like structure as a self -matrix [37–41]. Others, dual-emissive C-dots had an aggregation-induced RTP characteristic, the yellow RTP derived from a newly formed excited triplet state due to the aggregation among inter-C-dots [42]. Similar to the heavy atom effect of organic RTP dyes, the C-dots with heavy atoms can also be activated and improved the matrix-free RTP [43,44]. RTP C-dots are a good candidate for photosensitized oxygen activation [45–47]. RTP of solid C-dots disappears in aqueous solution because the energy of excited triplet state is transferred to dissolved oxygen, resulting in the formation of singlet state oxygen, which is a noted photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy. Among present photosensitizers, C-dots are very attractive due to their low cost, high stability, and good biocompatibility. Therefore, the pursuit of C-dots as highly efficient photosensitizers for photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy is of great significance.

In this study, isophthalic acid as a precursor was adopted to prepare C-dots (IPA-C-dots) through a one-pot hydrothermal approach. IPA-C-dots can emit, as well as blue fluorescence, long-lived green RTP in solid powder due to a small singlet-triplet energy gap (ΔEST), which enables to last for several seconds to the naked eye after 365 nm UV lamp was turned off. The TFC FO membranes were coupled with nothing, polylysine (PLL), IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites to prepare control membrane (Control-M), PLL one (PLL-M), and IPA-C-dots/PLL one (IPA-C-dots/PLL-M), respectively. Through the comparison of their performances in FO mode, IPA-C-dots/PLL-M was testified to greatly better the surface hydrophilicity, which implied the enhancement of membrane performances. Most significantly, IPA-C-dots aqueous solution can produce 1O2 that is a noted photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy reagent through energy transfer from excited triplet state to dissolved oxygen, which responded to the inhibition of bacterial proliferation ability and biofouling resistance properties of IPA-C-dots/PLL-M with high efficiency. Due to the good FO performance and excellent antibacterial property of IPA-CDs/PLL-M, the strategy on the coupling of RTP C-dots onto TFC FO membrane will be widely used in the field of water treatment and seawater desalination in the future.

The m-phenylenediamine (MPD, purity 99%) was the product of Macklin (Shanghai, China). Isophthalic acid (IPA, purity 99%), 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino) propyl] carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, purity 98%), N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, purity 99%), and 1,3,5-Benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, purity over 99%) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, purity 99%) and N–N-Dimethylacetamide (DMAC, purity 99%) were obtained from Tianjin BODI Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Escherichia coli (ATCC8739) was purchased from Shanghai Luwei Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All others were analytical reagent grade and purchased commercially and used as received.

Typically, 0.2g IPA was dissolved in 20 mL double deionized water (DDW) under ultrasonication for 30 min. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 250 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooled to room temperature, the solution was centrifugated at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large precipitates. The supernatant was freeze-dried to obtain white IPA-C-dots powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coupling PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL onto TFC FO membrane
ce:section.ce:para: IPA-C-dots were coupled onto the surface of TFC FO membranes through a hybrid technique. Firstly, EDC/NHS (4 mM) was dispersed in IPA-C-dots aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) at pH 6 by sonication for 15 min, and then the resulting solution was mixed with PLL (50 μg/mL) for 30 min to form IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites. Secondly, to conjugate IPA-C-dots/PLL onto the active layer of the TFC FO membrane, the membrane was sandwiched between a backing plate and a frame to expose the active layer only. A 200 ppm of sodium hypochlorite was utilized to etch the membrane surface for 2 min prior to wash with UP water. A 60 mM of EDC solution with pH 6.0 was dumped onto the membrane surface for 20 min, which can alter the carboxyl of PA active layer into amine-reactive esters. The EDC solution was replaced with IPA-C-dots/PLL aqueous solution and then reacted for 30 min. Hereafter, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M was washed with UP water for several times to remove the residual IPA-C-dots/PLL and stored in UP water at 10 °C for further use. Similarly, PLL modified TFC FO membrane was also prepared. Accordingly, the TFC FO membranes coupled with nothing, PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL denoted as control membrane (Control-M) (see Preparation of TFC FO membrane in Supplementary Information), PLL one (PLL-M), and IPA-C-dots/PLL one (IPA-C-dots/PLL-M), respectively, were prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of TFC FO membrane performance
ce:section.ce:para:
The performances of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were compared in FO mode [11,15,16]. TFC FO membrane with a 9.62 cm2 of effective surface area was used. UP water as feed solution for each experiment was conducted. The feed and draw solutions with a flow rate of 500 mL/min on both sides of the membrane were pushed by peristaltic pumps. A digital balance recorded the weight of water permeate as a function of time. The water flux (Jw, in units of Lm−2h−1, abbreviated as LMH) was measured by the volume change of permeate (eq (1)). The salt concentration can be calculated by measuring the conductivity in the feed solution. The reverse salt flux (Js, in units of gm−2h−1, abbreviated as gMH) was calculated by the salt concentration change of feed solution. Jw and Js were calculated by Eqs. (1) and (2) as follows:

The antibacterial effects of three types of membranes were assessed with ATCC8739 Escherichia coli as model bacterial strains. Before the experiment, all instruments were sterilized in an autoclave. Bacteria colonies were picked off with a swab and the strains were sub-cultured in 2 mL Luria Bertani (LB) broth media at 37 °C for 18h in a constant temperature incubator. 18 mL of phosphate buffered saline was added into LB broth media and shook to obtain bacterial suspension (106 CFU/mL), then store at 4°C. Prior to use, it was diluted with normal saline to obtain suspensions with different concentrations.

The antibacterial activities of the membranes were determined with a colony-counting method. Membranes (effective area 2.54 cm × 7.62 cm) were first sterilized with UV light for 10 min and placed on glass slides. Next, under light irradiation, the active side of the membranes was contacted with 100 μL of bacteria suspension of 103 CFU/mL followed by covering them with glass slides for 2h at 37°C. The membrane was then transferred into physiological saline and sonication for 5 min to remove deposited bacteria from the membrane surface. Finally, the bacteria suspension was spread on a nutrient agar plate, incubated for 24 h at 37 °C, and the colonies were counted. The antibacterial activity was calculated as below:

where N

The FO setup was firstly washed by 75% ethanol for 15 min, and then washed by DDW for 30 min. The membranes were installed and rinsed in DDW for 30 min followed by running for 1000 min using 2 M NaCl draw solution and fouling feed solution. 500 μL original bacteria suspension with 2.1 g LB broth powder was added into feed solution (700 mL DDW) to enhance the bacterial growth rate and accelerate the biological pollution on the membrane surface.

RTP C-dots possessed their significant properties as follows: 1) Raw materials are extremely cheap and easy to obtain. Different from the raw materials with high purity for the synthesis of metal-based doped quantum dots (QDs) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) are requisite, and the minority of rare elements used for QDs and UCNPs are expensive. The precursors used to the preparation of RTP C-dots, for example, phosphoric acid together with cholamine or ethanediamine [37,38], citric acid and polyethyleneimine [45], and other simple aromatic carbonyl compounds [41,42], have been reported and used without further purification; 2) Both synthesis and purification process are very simple. Natural biomass or chemical reagents were utilized to prepare RTP C-dots by a hydrothermal approach [33] or microwave pyrolysis [37,38], and via straightforward dialysis or extraction for purification. However, the troublesome separation and purification operations for synthesizing purely organic phosphors are necessary; 3) Inducing strategies are simple, even needless. As mentioned above, the aromatic C/PO groups were doped into the interior of C-dots, the polymer-like structure as self-matrices can activate afterglow of the C-dots [37,38]. When n-π* transition groups locate at the surface of the C-dots, the need of simple poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) [48,49], polyacrylamide [50], urea/biuret and melamine [51,52], zeolites [53,54], boric acid/boron cxynitride [55,56], and so on, embedded and immobilized the excited triplet state. Currently, the crystallization pathway was mainly focused on the activation of RTP of pure organic phosphors [57,58]. However, the development of pure organic RTP has been greatly limited by the uncontrollable growth process and stern growth conditions; 4) Surface chemistry is tunable. The excited-state dynamics of C-dots can be significantly influenced by impurity doping, which led to an extended π-conjugation and a prolonged lifetime [55,56]. Therefore, it is highly demanded for the revolutionary application of RTP C-dots.

In the present work, IPA as a precursor was adopted to prepare C-dots. The formation of the polymer-like composites firstly took place among IPA molecules. The polymer-like composites were subsequently carbonized into IPA-C-dots (see Scheme 1 a). The morphology, surface charged property, and crystalline state of the IPA-C-dots were characterized by TEM, zeta-potential and XRD, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 a, well-dispersed nanoparticles can be observed from the TEM image with an average size of about 3.2 nm in diameter, and the statistical particle size of IPA-C-dots was distributed in the range of 2.1–5.2 nm (data not shown). The Zeta-potential was measured as −12.4 eV, indicating the IPA-C-dots are negatively charged, which is possibly caused by the –COOH groups anchored on the surface of the IPA-C-dots (see Supplementary Fig. 1). To further analyze the structural characteristics of IPA-C-dots, an XRD pattern was used. The result shown in Fig. 2 b reveals that the diffraction peak at 27.1° and a shoulder one at 38.5°, which corresponded to the crystal planes (002) (d002 = 0.36 nm) and (100) of carbon [59], respectively, implying that the IPA-C-dots are essentially amorphous, which may be caused by the attachment of carboxyl groups on the carbon surface [42].

The FTIR spectra were usually performed to identify functional groups on the surface of the C-dots, which can predict their application potentials. The chemical structure and formation of IPA-C-dots were elucidated by comparing the FTIR spectra of IPA-C-dots and the precursor IPA. As shown in Fig. 1c, the evident differences in the ranges from 600 to 1600 cm−1 and 2500 to 3200 cm−1, indicating the formation of new species, namely C-dots, were affirmed after the carbonization of IPA. In addition, compared with the curve of IPA, the obviously reduced absorptions at 2540-3089 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibrations), 1682 cm−1 (CO stretching vibrations), 1270 cm−1 (C–O stretching vibrations), 1416 cm−1 (in-plane bending vibrations of –OH on aromatic carboxyl groups), and 914 cm−1 (out-of-plane bending vibrations of –OH on aromatic carboxyl groups) on the curve of IPA-C-dots were observed [42], implying the loss of carboxyl groups. Reversely, the obviously enhanced absorptions at 1554, 1434, and 1378 cm−1 (CC stretching vibrations) imply the production of more sp2 structures in the IPA-C-dots [42].

Also, the XPS was employed to further confirm the chemical composition and surface functional groups of the IPA-C-dots. The survey scan curve exhibits the IPA-C-dots compose of C and O elements, the corresponding binding peaks located at 285.0 and 399.5 eV, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum displays three peaks at 284.7, 285.3, and 288.5 eV (see Fig. 1e), which can be ascribed to CC, C–O, and –COOH groups, respectively [60–63]. The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum is fitted to be two peaks at 531.4 and 533.0 eV that can be assigned to CO and C–O bonds, respectively [42,60,63] (see Fig. 1f). Therefore, a large amount of –COOH anchoring on the surface of C-dots revealed by the FTIR and XPS spectra gave the surface state of IPA-C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and antibacterial properties of IPA-C-dots

ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the IPA-C-dots in solid powder including solid UV–vis, afterglow, and excitation-dependent emission, were firstly investigated here. It can be seen from the normalized solid UV–vis spectrum, the obvious absorption peaks covering total ultraviolet to visible region were observed. The strong absorption locates below 400 nm, and the shoulder absorption, greater than 400 nm. The corresponding band gap energy was calculated to be ~3.39 eV (see Supplementary Fig. 2). The excitation-dependent emissive behavior was studied here. The maximum excitation at ~360 nm (see Supplementary Fig. 3) and the corresponding maximum emission at ~~508 nm were observed on the phosphorescence spectra of solid powder of IPA-C-dots (see Fig. 2a). The time-resolved decay curve of the IPA-C-dots obtained by single-photon counting method was fitted well to a tri-exponent nature with 15.8 ms of an average lifetime (see Fig. 2b and Supplementary Table 1). Significantly, the C-dots powder can emit green afterglow for ~9s to naked eye until it gets dark after the 365 nm lamp was turned off (see Fig. 2c).

The exploration of the origin of RTP is of the essence. Initially, aromatic CO and CN moieties with long-lived n-π* transition at the surface of C-dots were considered as the root of RTP emission due to their strong spin-orbit coupling [33,34]. The RTP of IPA-C-dots was activated without any matrices used. No heavy elements were testified to be doped into IPA-C-dots. So the explanations of matrix inducting and heavy atom effect for C-dots-based matrix-free RTP are unreasonable. When C/PO moieties were capped into the interior of C-dots, the polymer-like structure as self-matrices can activate RTP [37,38], which seemed to make clear the RTP mechanism of IPA-C-dots. However, the decarboxylation reaction easily took place among IPA molecules to condense into aromatic rings at high temperature, the residual carboxyl groups anchored on the surface of C-dots by and large [42]. This also failed in the explanation of RTP of IPA-C-dots. Usually, a lower energy gap (ΔE ST) between the lowest singlet (S1) and triplet (T1) excited states of organic phosphors can activate RTP. The ΔE ST of IPA-C-dots was quantitatively measured to be 0.47 eV, which is unfavorable for an effective intersystem crossing (ISC) process [33,65]. A much lower temperature is usually in favor of the protection of triplet excitons due to the reinforcement of various weak interactions. The effect of temperature on RTP emission was also considered. The emission took on converse change with temperature, that is, temperature rose from liquid nitrogen temperature (77 K) to room one (298 K), the RTP intensity correspondingly decreased (see Supplementary Fig. 4). Therefore, the low ΔE ST well explained the origin of RTP of IPA-C-dots (vide post).

The fluorescence properties of IPA-C-dots aqueous solution such as UV–vis absorption, maximum excitation/emission wavelength, lifetime, excitation-dependent emission, and quantum yield (QY) were simply studied here. As displayed in Supplementary Fig. 5a, two UV–Vis absorptions at ~280 and ~315 nm were observed on the UV–Vis absorption spectrum curve, which may be attributed to π–π* transition and n–π* transition, respectively [61,64]. In addition, that the maximal excitation and emission at 310 and 407 nm, respectively, can be seen. The time-correlated decay curve was fitted well to a tri-exponent nature with a mean lifetime 4.0 ns (see Supplementary Fig. 5b), which is far lower than that of phosphorescence. With the excitation wavelength change from 250 to 370 nm, the maximum excitation wavelength at 310 nm was observed (see Supplementary Fig. 6a). The QY of the IPA-C-dots was detected as ~12.6% using quinine sulfate as a reference under 365 nm UV light (see Supplementary Fig. 6b).

Also, the antibacterial property of IPA-C-dots is the emphasis. Carbon-based nanozymes have attracted extensive attention due to their low cost, high activity, outstanding stability, and extreme importance for potential photodynamic antibacterial application [66,67]. Therefore, the IPA-C-dots as a photosensitizer for photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy were investigated. E. coli. was co-cultured with and without 12 mg/L of IPA-C-dots under light irradiation for 1.0 h, then plated for the visualization, and the latter was utilized for comparison. As shown in Fig. 2d, the number of bacterial colonies with 12 mg/L of IPA-C-dots in the light irradiated plates (see Fig. 2d-right) was far less than that in light only (see Fig. 2d-left). Via quantitative assay, the antibacterial efficiency of IPA-C-dots over 92% was determined, revealing the IPA-C-dots have great potential as an excellent antibacterial reagent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of TFC FO membrane coupled with IPA-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Biofouling has become a concerning issue in membrane technologies. The attachment of microorganisms to the membrane surface gave rise to an adverse effect on membrane life span, resulting in advance of operating and maintenance costs. In the face of challenge, many attempts have therefore been made to exploit advances in materials science to obtain anti-biofouling membranes for bettering their bactericidal properties and then suppressing the activity of attached organisms in order to minimize biofilm formation [68]. In light of the strong antibacterial potential of IPA-C-dots, it was naturally expected to couple them on the surface of the TFC FO membrane to better its antifouling performance. Two approaches were attempted: 1) IPA-C-dots were firstly conjugated to PPL (see Scheme 1b), then formed IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites were coupled onto the active layer of TFC FO membrane (see Scheme 1c); 2) PLL was firstly conjugated onto the active layer of TFC FO membrane (see Supplementary Scheme 1a), then IPA-C-dots were coupled onto the active layer of TFC FO membrane (see Supplementary Scheme 1b). EDC/NHS as a coupling reagent ran through all processes. The following experiments were testified the approach 2 is impracticable. The possible reason lies in positively charged PLL was conjugated onto negatively charged skin layer, resulting in PLL lodged on the active layer through electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bond, etc. The few residual –NH2 of PLL can combine with few IPA-C-dots, the inferior antibacterial effect was observed. Therefore, approach 1 was adopted to conjugate IPA-C-dots onto the active layer of the TFC FO membrane.

The TFC FO membranes Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were characterized by fluorescence/phosphorescence spectra, zeta-potential, FTIR, contact angle, and AFM. The active layer owns aromatic rings, so three types of membranes can emit fluorescence. But fluorescence intensity of IPA-C-dots/PLL-M is stronger than those of Control-M and PLL-M due to the conjugation of IPA-C-dots onto Control-M (see Fig. 3 a). Similarly, the enhanced RTP on IPA-C-dots/PLL-M was observed (see Fig. 3b). Both demonstrated IPA-C-dots were successfully conjugated onto PLL to form IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites. Also, these nanocomposites were successfully coupled onto the active layer of the TFC FO membrane. The zeta-potential can provide the information of the surface charge distribution of three types of membranes along with pH change. As shown in Fig. 3c, when the pH value was changed from 4 to 9, the zeta-potential of Control-M was negative, revealing much –COOH on the active layer of the membrane. PLL as a cationic polymer, the zeta-potential of PLL-M was significantly changed from with an isoelectric point at pH ~7.0 or so, which indicated PLL-M has a net –COOH. Compared with the Control-M, IPA-C-dots/PLL-M took on much more negative zeta-potential than Control-M, giving the same results as fluorescence/phosphorescence spectra. The reason for this fact can be explained as PLL is positively charged, but IPA-C-dots, reversely charged, led to IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites integrally negatively charged.

The ATR-FTIR was carried on to learn the changes of the functional groups of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M. The results shown in Fig. 3d mainly display the peaks change of the amide bonds on three types of membrane. The peaks at 1660 and 1542 cm−1 can be assigned to CO stretch, N–H bending, and C–N stretching of amide, respectively [69]. The peak at 1610 cm−1 represents the deformation stretching of the N–H bond in amine groups [70]. The successful coupling of PLL or IPA-C-dots/PLL on the membrane surface is evidenced by the signal enhancement of CO band, and N–H and C–N ones. This can be ascribed to the amidation reactions between the amino group of PLL and carboxyl of activated TFC FO membrane surface and that of IPA-C-dots.

The variation in hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity after modification plays a key role in characterizing the improvement of the wettability of the TFC FO membrane. The surface of Control-M showed an 86.64° of contact angle, indicating its high hydrophobic nature. After coupling PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL onto Control-M, The surface hydrophilicity of the PLL-M and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were both bettered, the corresponding contact angles are 67.94, and 53.87, respectively (see Fig. 4 a). Simply, the surface modifications by PLL with abundant polar amine groups and IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites with abundant carboxylic acid functional groups, as well as amine groups, may result in enhanced surface hydrophilicity. Therefore, the contact angle decreased along with the incorporation of PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL onto Control-M.

AFM was used to compare the roughness profile of the surface of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M. The results shown in Fig. 4b indicate that the surface roughness of Control-M was relatively uniform (153 nm, Ra). The PLL-M does not show a significant change of the roughness of the etched surface but slight enhancement (166 nm, Ra), which, on the one hand, suggested the PLL was conformally attached to the membrane surface, on the other hand, formed a monolayer which followed the rough substrate configuration. What is more, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M exhibited a much smoother surface topography with a 105 nm (Ra). The smoother surface morphology of the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M can be attributed to the IPA-C-dots occupying the valleys of the conjugated PLL. Also, the smoother surface morphology implies fewer binding sites for bacterial adhesion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of coupling RTP C-dots on TFC FO membrane performance
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, the improvement of surface hydrophilicity will result in the enhancement of separation performance of the TFC FO membrane. FO performances of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were investigated here. The results illustrated in Fig. 5 a reveal that, in comparison to the other two membranes, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M exhibit relatively higher water fluxes. This behavior can be mainly ascribed to the enhanced hydrophilicity and is in good agreement with contact angle results. In addition, when DI water as a feed solution and 2 M NaCl as a draw solution was utilized, the Js of Control-M was 9.1 gMH. The increased Js of PLL-M and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M possibly resulted from the apparent swelling of the membranes in a high salt concentration environment (draw solution) with the continuous operation of the FO process (see Fig. 5b), leading to the formation of loose structures adapted to salt ion diffusion along with an increase in ICP [71].

Intrinsic transport properties of three pieces of TFC FO membranes were also evaluated (see Calculation of intrinsic transport parameters in Supplementary Information). The corresponding results were listed in Table 1 , which shows a good accordance with the FO results. Compared with the water permeabilities (A value) of the Control-M (1.66 LMH/Bar) and PLL-M (1.72 LMH/Bar), the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M displayed slightly higher water permeability (1.77 LMH/Bar). The parameters (B, B/A), where B is salt permeability, and corresponding B/A ratios, (0.26, 0.16) for Control-M, (0.51, 0.30) for PLL-M, and (0.46, 0.26) for IPA-C-dots/PLL-M, were calculated, giving the similar trend with the FO results. In addition, the structural parameter (S) of Control-M is higher than those of the other two, which suggested the conjugations of hydrophilic PLL or IPA-C-dots/PLL onto TFC FO membrane are beneficial to reduce S, implying the weakening of ICP. Through RO mode, the salt rejection, 94.28% for Control-M, 89.7% for PLL-M, and 90.88% for IPA-C-dots/PLL-M, were determined, suggesting salt rejection is acceptable after coupling IPA-C-dots/PLL onto the TFC FO membrane. Compared with the commercialized CTA membranes and TFC ones recently reported, IPA-C-dots/PLL-M displayed a relatively excellent FO performance (see Supplementary Table 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the membranes antibacterial activity

ce:section.ce:para: E. coli was served as model bacteria to evaluate the antibacterial activities of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M. Fig. 6 shows the bacteria grown on agar plates of the different membranes. Among these, widespread E. coli on the agar plate of Control-M was observed. In comparison to Control-M, the agar plate of PLL-M showed a certain amount of E. coli less than that of Control-M, which offered the enhanced antibacterial activity (44%), demonstrating PLL-M enabled to antibacteria due to the cations of PLL. Most significantly, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M exhibited the strongest antibacterial activity (100%). The result indicates IPA-C-dots coupled on the membrane possessed excellent antibacterial ability except for the contribution of residual cation of PLL. The dramatically decreased bacterial survival on the IPA- C-dots/PLL-M can be attributed to the excellent photosensitized property of IPA-C-dots for the generation of singlet oxygen (vide post).

Also, the dynamic antibacterial activities of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were studied under light irradiation (see Scheme 1d). The water fluxes in biofouling experiments for Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were plotted in Fig. 7 a, each membrane maintained stable water flux for a period of time prior to steadily decline water flux. The declines in membrane water flux can be attributed to the growth of bacteria on a membrane surface [72]. The water flux of IPA-C-dots/PLL-M is always higher than that of the other two, and the water flux of PLL-M is over that of Control-M, which was observed during the whole testing process. This result is consistent with FO performance and intrinsic transport properties of membranes (see Fig. 5 and Table 1), in which the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M owned the highest Jw and water permeability.

The normalized fluxes (Jw/Jw0) caused by biofouling of Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M were given in Fig. 7 b. The water flux of the Control-M and PLL-M began to decrease from 200 min to 300 min, respectively. Meanwhile, the water flux of the IPA-CDs/PLL-M began to decrease until 450 min, which can be assigned to the antibacterial activities of PLL and IPA-C-dots/PLL, and the antibacterial activity of IPA-C-dots/PLL better than that of PLL [73]. This result is in good agreement with the static antibacterial test (see Fig. 6) and AFM (see Fig. 4b), in which the IPA-CDs/PLL-M possessed the highest antibacterial properties and the smoothest surface morphology, leading to the improvement of anti-biofouling property and the reduction of the interfacial adhesion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The possible mechanisms of antibacterial and membrane performance enhancement
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the static and dynamic anti-fouling performances with high efficiency of IPA-C-dots aqueous solution, it is of great importance for exploring the possible antibacterial mechanism of IPA-C-dots. On the one hand, the poor overlap between the HOMO and LUMO of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) states usually can decrease ΔEST and thus enhance ISC processes [74]. Therefore, ICT states introduced into molecules with dominant π–π* transitions may serve to bridge the large ΔEST, resulting in RTP under suitable conditions (see Fig. 8 a). On the other hand, if the structure of IPA-C-dots is compact and can efficiently prevent the dissolved oxygen from touching the excited triplet states. The excitons may relax by irradiation but energy transfer to dissolved oxygen to form 1O2 enables photodynamic antibacterial chemotherapy. Our previous report indicated that the interior of C-dots obtained by the “bottom-up” approach is an incompact structure, resulting in the RTP that can be observed in solid powder is invisible in an aqueous solution [60]. In fact, we cannot observe the green RTP in IPA-C-dots aqueous solution. Do the triplet excitons photosensitize dissolved oxygen to form 1O2? For this purpose, the EPR was conducted to answer this issue. As shown in Fig. 8b, The EPR spectra exhibit the much stronger 1O2 triplet signals with IPA-C-dots in the TEMP aqueous solution, verifying the antibacterial mechanism of IPA-C-dots [75]. As for the essence of membrane performance enhancement, the conjugation of IPA-C-dots/PLL onto Control-M greatly bettered the surface hydrophilicity of membrane, implying the integral improvement of membrane performance and biofouling resistance properties.

In summary, we have successfully prepared Control-M, PLL-M, and IPA-C-dots/PLL-M through a hybrid technique. IPA-C-dots/PLL-M displayed a remarkable enhancement in surface hydrophilicity and smoothness of polyamide layer after the coupling IPA-C-dots/PLL nanocomposites onto Control-M. The performances of three pieces of membranes were tested and compared in FO mode. The results indicate that the water permeability of IPA-C-dots/PLL-M is higher than those of the other two, and S parameter, much smaller. Most importantly, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M exhibited excellent antibacterial properties, resulting in the benefit of reduced loss of water flux under biofouling conditions and extended membrane lifetime. Consequently, the IPA-C-dots/PLL-M has a great potential to be applied in industrial FO membrane filtration in the future due to synthesis simplicity, cost cheapness, efficiency height, and operation easiness.

Wenfei Miao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Xia Chen and Shibiao Wu: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Qingchun Zhao: Validation. Zhaoming Liu and Tongwen Xu: Supervision.
